Title: PrismQuant: Rate-Distortion-Optimal Vector Quantization for Gaussian-Mixture Sources

## PrismQuant: RateÔÇôDistortion-Optimal Vector Quantization for Gaussian-Mixture Sources 

Bumsu Park 

Department of Electrical Engineering POSTECH Pohang, South Korea 

bumsupark@postech.ac.kr 

Chanho Park 

Department of Electrical Engineering POSTECH Pohang, South Korea 

chanho26@postech.ac.kr 

Youngmok Park 

Department of Electrical Engineering POSTECH Pohang, South Korea 

ympark1999@postech.ac.kr 

Namyoon Lee ÔłŚ

Department of Electrical Engineering POSTECH Pohang, South Korea 

nylee@postech.ac.kr 

Abstract 

For a Gaussian source under mean-squared error (MSE), classical transform coding is rateÔÇôdistortion (RD) optimal: the KarhunenÔÇôLo├Ęve transform (KLT) diagonal-izes the covariance, reverse waterfilling allocates the bits, and scalar quantization closes the loop. This elegant story breaks down for multimodal sources, where no single covariance can capture heterogeneous local geometries and the RD function loses its closed form. We revisit this problem through Gaussian-mixture sources and develop a constructive RD theory for them. Our key finding is that mixture structure costs only a component label . Conditioned on the active mixture compo-nent, each branch is Gaussian; the challenge is allocating bits across heterogeneous branches. We prove that the genie-aided conditional RD function is governed by a single global reverse-waterfilling level shared across all components and eigenmodes. Building on this result, we introduce P RISM QUANT , which transmits the component label losslessly and encodes the residual using the component-matched KLT followed by scalar quantization, achieving a rate within H(C)/n 

bits per source dimension of the converse with a vanishing asymptotic gap. We further develop a practical implementation based on EM-driven Gaussian-mixture learning, component-adaptive KLTs, and entropy-constrained scalar quantization (ECSQ). Experiments on synthetic Gaussian mixtures show that P RISM QUANT 

closely approaches the theoretical RD bound, while experiments on real-world channel-state-information (CSI) data demonstrate competitive or superior perfor-mance compared with transformer-based learned codecs at more than one order of magnitude smaller model size. 

1 Introduction 

ShannonÔÇÖs rateÔÇôdistortion (RD) theorem specifies the minimum rate required to represent a source at a prescribed distortion [ 1ÔÇô 3]. For high-dimensional sources, however, the theorem is largely existential: under mean-squared error (MSE), the Gaussian source remains the only mainstream setting aadmitting an explicit, constructive RD characterization via the KarhunenÔÇôLo├Ęve transform (KLT) and reverse waterfilling [ 2, 4]. This classical framework breaks down for multimodal sources, 

> ÔłŚCorresponding author. 40th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2026).

where a single covariance structure cannot adequately capture heterogeneous local geometries, and the exact RD function has remained open since BergerÔÇÖs 1971 monograph [2]. 

Setting. We focus on the most analytically tractable and operationally meaningful class of multi-modal sources: Gaussian mixtures . We use X Ôłł Rn to denote the random source vector and x Ôłł Rn

a generic realization. The source distribution is

The Gaussian-mixture model (GMM) class is far less restrictive than it first appears : Gaussian mixtures are universal density approximators [ 5 ], meaning that any continuous integrable density can be approximated well in L1 distance by a sufficiently rich mixture (Lemma 1). Consequently, a constructive RD theory for (1) naturally extends to arbitrary continuous multimodal sources by combining expectationÔÇômaximization (EM) mixture learning with the proposed coding framework. 

The CSI compression as a motivating application. Compression of high-dimensional multimodal sources is a fundamental challenge in modern data-driven systems. A particularly important ex-ample arises in frequency-division-duplex (FDD) massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) orthogonal-frequency-division-multiplexing (OFDM) wireless systems, where accurate channel-state information (CSI) feedback is critical for reliable communication [ 6, 7]. Since CSI is inherently high-dimensional due to large antenna arrays and many subcarriers, compression is unavoidable under strict bandwidth constraints. While CSI under fixed propagation conditions is well approximated by a correlated Gaussian model [ 8 ], realistic CSI aggregated across users and environments exhibits strongly multimodal structure, naturally motivating Gaussian-mixture modeling [ 9]. In this regime, single-covariance KLT coding becomes fundamentally mismatched, whereas modern learned CSI codecs [ 10 , 11 ] often rely on large black-box neural architectures. P RISM QUANT addresses this gap through an explicit constructive RD theory together with a low-complexity and interpretable coding framework. 

Contributions. (i) First explicit constructive RD characterization for Gaussian-mixture sources. We extend the explicit, constructive RD theory, previously known only for single-covariance Gaussian sources, to general Gaussian mixtures via a converse-achievability sandwich . The resulting converseÔÇôachievability gap is exactly the amortized label entropy H(C)/n and vanishes asymptoti-cally whenever the mixture order satisfies K = 2 o(n). By Gaussian-mixture universality, the result further lifts to arbitrary multimodal sources through EM-based mixture fitting. Conditional source coding [ 2 , 3 ] provides a general theoretical framework, but obtaining an explicit RD characterization together with a constructive code for multimodal sources has remained elusive. Why has this been hard? The difficulty stems from the fact that the unconditional Gaussian-mixture density admits no tractable entropy characterization; even sharp bounds and closed-form approximations for mixture entropy are themselves nontrivial [ 12 ]. Our key idea is to augment the source with the latent com-ponent label, converting the non-Gaussian entropy into a sum of Gaussian entropies plus an exact label cost H(C). This yields a separable convex optimization whose Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions induce a single global reverse-waterfilling level shared across all mixture components. 

(ii) PrismQuant codec. We propose P RISM QUANT , a practical two-stage losslessÔÇôlossy coding framework for Gaussian-mixture sources that attains the achievability bound under correct label detection. The codec first learns the underlying Gaussian-mixture distribution via expectationÔÇô maximization (EM), estimates the component label through maximum-a-posteriori (MAP) inference, and transmits the label losslessly. Conditioned on the detected component, P RISM QUANT then applies the component-matched KLT followed by entropy-constrained scalar quantization (ECSQ) and entropy coding across the resulting eigenmodes. The entire pipeline admits closed-form operations at every stage and yields a fully practical bitstream-level implementation with low complexity and strong interpretability. 

(iii) One global reverse-waterfilling level. Both the converse and the achievability are governed by a single global reverse-waterfilling level ╬╝Ôőć shared across the eigenmodes of all mixture components. 2The Gaussian-mixture RD problem therefore collapses onto the same one-dial allocation structure as classical single-covariance Gaussian transform coding (TC), despite the presence of heterogeneous eigenbases. This is fundamentally different from prior classified TC methods such as WUTC [13 ], which use independent per-component water levels ╬╝Ôőćc and therefore cannot perform globally optimal rate allocation with respect to the full mixture prior. In contrast, the shared water level in PRISM QUANT induces more practical cross-component eigenmode pruning and yields the genie-aided conditional RD characterization up to the amortized label cost H(C)/n .

(iv) Validation on synthetic mixtures and real-world CSI data. On synthetic Gaussian-mixture sources, we sweep the mixture order K at fixed source dimension n and show that P RISM QUANT 

with MAP-estimated component labels closely tracks both the genie-aided oracle and the theoretical RD bound across varying K, while single-covariance TC exhibits an increasingly severe mismatch gap as multimodality grows. On the real-world DeepMIMO dataset [ 14 ], P RISM QUANT substantially improves over classical TC and achieves competitive reconstruction performance against learned CSI compression schemes including CsiNet [10] and Swin-NTC [11], while requiring one to two orders of magnitude smaller model size. 

2 Related Works 

We organize prior work into four lines and clarify, line by line, how P RISM QUANT differs from each. A point-by-point architectural comparison is deferred to Appendix F. 

Conditional and side-information source coding. Conditional rateÔÇôdistortion theory [ 15 , 2], WynerÔÇôZiv coding [ 16 ], and HeegardÔÇôBerger coding [ 17 ] provide general theoretical frameworks for structured compression problems. These frameworks characterize the limits but do not yield an explicit closed-form RD function for the multimodal source classes targeted in this paper; P RIS -

> M

QUANT can be read as a constructive specialization that supplies such a closed form for Gaussian mixtures. 

Classified vector quantization and weighted universal transform coding (WUTC). The closest architectural ancestor of P RISM QUANT is the line of classified VQ [ 18 ] and Weighted Universal Transform Coding [ 19 , 13 ]. These schemes also transmit a latent component label losslessly and then encode the residual using a component-matched KarhunenÔÇôLo├Ęve transform (KLT) plus scalar quantization. There are two structural differences. First , the branch-selection rule: WUTC selects the active class at the encoder by an exhaustive rateÔÇôdistortion search that encodes the block under every candidate transform and keeps the best, whereas P RISM QUANT uses a Bayes-optimal MAP classifier bC(x) = arg max c ¤ÇcN (x; ╬╝c, ╬úc) derived from the fitted Gaussian-mixture source model. This replaces K encode-and-measure evaluations per block with a single Mahalanobis-distance comparison, and identifies the branch entropy with the latent-label entropy H(C). Second , the rate-allocation rule: WUTC uses an independent reverse-waterfilling level ╬╝Ôőćc per component, whereas PRISM QUANT uses a single global water level ╬╝Ôőć shared across all components and eigenmodes (Theorem 1). Together, these two changes convert a heuristic multi-codebook codec without a matching converse into one that attains the genie-aided RD function up to the amortized label cost 

H(C)/n (Theorem 3). Closely related Gaussian-mixture VQ schemes [ 20 , 21 ] share the multi-codebook spirit but likewise lack both a probabilistic selection rule and a converseÔÇôachievability sandwich, and the classical single-covariance Gaussian transform coder [ 2, 4] is recovered as the special case K = 1 .

Adaptive multiple transform (AMT) in standardized video codecs. A standardized, hand-designed instance of the library-of-transforms idea is the adaptive multiple transform (AMT)ÔÇöalso called Multiple Transform Selection (MTS)ÔÇöadopted in HEVC and VVC. The concept was first introduced by Han, Saxena, and Rose [ 22 , 23 ] as a joint optimization of spatial prediction and block transform; it was extended into a mode-dependent DCT/DST coding scheme by Saxena and Fernandes [ 24 ], generalized as enhanced multiple transform by Zhao et al. [ 25 ], and standardized in VVC [ 26 ]. AMT also signals a branch index, but its library consists of a small number of fixed trigonometric transforms (DCT-II together with DST-VII and DCT-VIII variants), the branch is chosen by an encoder-side rateÔÇôdistortion search, and the analysis is purely operational. P RISM QUANT differs from AMT in three respects: (i) its library is derived from a fitted Gaussian-mixture source model rather than hand-designed; (ii) the branch is selected by a Bayes-optimal MAP classifier rather 3than by an RD search; and (iii) the bit allocation is summarized by a single global water level with a matching closed-form converse, instead of by per-block quantization parameters without an information-theoretic gap analysis. 

Neural compression and RD estimators. Modern learned compressors specialize expressive neural architectures to specific source classes (e.g., image [ 27 , 28 ] and CSI [ 10 , 11 ]). Their RD behavior is characterized empirically through training objectives and benchmark curves rather than through explicit coding laws, and plug-in or neural RD estimators [ 29 ] likewise provide numerical bounds rather than constructive codes. Source-agnostic vector quantization such as TurboQuant [ 30 ] is data-oblivious by design and aims at worst-case rather than source-adaptive performance. P RISM QUANT 

occupies the complementary corner of the design space: source-adaptive but driven by a probabilistic source model rather than by a learned black box, with an EM-driven Gaussian-mixture fit [ 5, 31 ], MAP-based component-label inference, component-matched KLTs, and entropy-constrained scalar quantization (ECSQ). 

3 Gaussian-Mixture Source and Universality 

Problem setup. We consider an independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) random vector source {Xt}tÔëą1 with Xt Ôłł Rn drawn from the real Gaussian mixture (1) ; xt Ôłł Rn denotes a generic realization. A blocklength-m, rate-R source code is a pair of mappings fm : ( Rn)m Ôćĺ{1, . . . , 2mnR } and gm : {1, . . . , 2mnR } Ôćĺ (Rn)m, where R is in bits per source dimension. The fidelity criterion is normalized MSE (NMSE) D = 1

Pmt=1 EÔłąXt Ôłĺ ╦ćXtÔłą22. The operational RD function RÔőć(D) is the infimum of all achievable rates at distortion D [1ÔÇô3]. 

Why Gaussian mixtures? Many real-world sources are well modeled as locally Gaussian within a homogeneous regime, but globally non-stationary across regimes (e.g., channel responses across propagation geometries [ 32 , 33 ], image patches across textures [ 34 , 35 ], semantic features across classes [ 28 , 36 ], and speech or acoustic features across speakers [ 37 ]). A Gaussian mixture replaces the (intractable) integral over the latent regime by a finite collection of representative covariances, each conditionally Gaussian. By Lemma 1 below, the model is universal: any continuous integrable density admits an arbitrarily accurate Gaussian-mixture surrogate. 

Lemma 1 (Universality of Gaussian mixtures [ 5]) . For any continuous integrable density p on Rd

and any ╬Á > 0, there exist K, weights {¤Çc}Kc=1 , means {╬╝c}Kc=1 , and covariances {╬úc}Kc=1 such that

Composing Lemma 1 with P RISM QUANT gives a constructive codec for an arbitrary continuous multimodal source: learn the parameters of Gaussian mixture distribution by EM and feed it to the encoder. The explicit RD theory below is stated for the finite mixture (1) , but its operational reach is the entire class of continuous multimodal densities.

The lower endpoint h( X| C) is the genie-aided benchmark in which the active component is known for free; the upper endpoint corresponds to (1) lossless label coding and (2) component-matched lossy transform coding . This is the organizing principle of P RISM QUANT : the discrete, multimodal part of the source is absorbed into a low-rate label stream, leaving a textbook Gaussian transform-coding problem within the selected branch. 4 The PrismQuant Codec 

PRISM QUANT is a practical hybrid losslessÔÇôlossy coding framework for the Gaussian-mixture source (1) . The mixture parameters ╬Ş = {(¤Çc, ╬╝ c, R c)}Kc=1 are learned offline (e.g., EM on training samples) and stored as a shared probabilistic dictionary at both the encoder and decoder; only the component label and the coded transform coefficients are transmitted online. The entire pipeline admits closed-form operations at every stage and yields a fully practical bitstream-level implementation.

(5) Scalar quantization and entropy coding: active coefficients ( ╬╗╦ćc,i > ╬╝ ) are quantized at the allocated rate using ECSQ [ 38 ], while inactive coefficients are set to zero. (6) Reconstruction: the decoder reconstructs ╦ćx = ╬╝╦ćc + U╦ćc ╦ćs. The overall encoding and decoding pipeline of P RISM QUANT is illustrated in Figure 1. 

Cross-component pruning. Because the same global water level ╬╝ thresholds the eigenvalues of every component, eigenmodes satisfying ╬╗c,i ÔëĄ ╬╝ need not be stored, transmitted, or processed during runtime. Consequently, P RISM QUANT retains only the active eigenmodes that receive nonzero rate allocation under reverse waterfilling, significantly reducing both the effective model footprint and the coding complexity. Classical Gaussian reverse waterfilling performs pruning only within a single fixed eigenbasis; in contrast, the multimodal setting induces pruning jointly across heterogeneous component-dependent eigenbases under a single global allocation parameter. The resulting runtime memory and per-vector encode/decode complexity therefore scale as ╬ś( nL c(╬╝)) ,where Lc(╬╝) = |{ i : ╬╗c,i > ╬╝ }| denotes the number of active eigenmodes in component c.

Why one water level suffices: the bathtub view. For a multimodal source, the textbook intuition is one component-specific water level per component plus another to balance the component budgets. Theorem 1 shows that this is unnecessary: every extra bit spent on component-eigenmode pair (c, i )

is both paid and rewarded with probability ¤Çc, so the prior cancels in the KKT condition and a single Lagrange multiplier ╬╝ governs the entire mixture. Figure 3 visualizes this as a bathtub: each 

(c, i ) is a rectangular column of height ╬╗c,i and width ¤Çc/n , and a single horizontal line at height ╬╝

decides simultaneously (i) the active set across all K components, (ii) the per-eigenmode distortion 

dÔőćc,i = min {╬╗c,i , ╬╝ } (equalized to ╬╝ on active eigenmodes by KKT), and (iii) the per-eigenmode rate rÔőćc,i = [ 12 log 2(╬╗c,i /╬╝ )] +. The same ╬╝ arising in the conditional benchmark also determines PRISM QUANT ÔÇÖs on-line allocation, so the codec is parameterized by a single scalar dial regardless of

Consequently, for sufficiently high-dimensional sources, the MAP label error probability decays exponentially with dimension, causing practical P RISM QUANT to approach the genie-aided benchmark. More details are provided in Appendix E. 

6 Experiments 

Setup. We evaluate P RISM QUANT on synthetic mixture sources and on the DeepMIMO [14] CSI dataset. For synthetic sources, weights {¤Çc}Kc=1 are drawn uniformly from the simplex; component variances are drawn from [0 .1, 10 .0] ; means are zero; and Uc is the QR factor of a random Gaussian matrix. For each configuration, more than 100,000 samples are drawn from the mixture and rateÔÇô distortion is swept by varying ╬╝ over 50 logarithmically-spaced points in [10 Ôłĺ5, 10 1].For the DeepMIMO evaluation the dimensionality of CSI is parameterized with the number of antennas Nt and the number of subcarriers Nc. Since the stacked CSI vector h Ôłł CNtNc is too high-dimensional for direct GMM fitting via EM, we apply a preprocessing step that first separates the real and imaginary parts of h and concatenates them into a real-valued vector of length 2NtNc,which is then partitioned into non-overlapping sub-vectors of length n, yielding Ôîł2NtNc/n Ôîë sub-vectors per CSI realization. Each n-dimensional sub-vector x Ôłł Rn is then modeled by the Gaussian mixture. The dictionary ╬Ş = {(¤Çc, ╬╝ c, R c)}Kc=1 is learned by EM on the training split and frozen for inference; the label is amortized over a ¤ä -vector coherence window, and since users in DeepMIMO remain stationary we take ¤ä Ôćĺ Ôł× , so that the label cost vanishes and the effective rate reduces to 

H(C)/(¤ä n ) Ôćĺ 0.We compare four methods: the Theoretical (lower bound) Rcond (D) (Theorem 2, computed via reverse waterfilling without label overhead), the Theoretical (upper bound) RPQ (╬╝) (Theorem 3), the Operational (Genie) curve obtained by running P RISM QUANT in Section 4 with oracle labels, and the Operational (MAP) curve obtained with MAP-inferred labels. Additional EM implementation details are deferred to Appendix D. 

Effect of K on synthetic mixtures. Figure 4 shows rateÔÇôdistortion curves at fixed source dimension 

n = 32 and mixture orders K Ôłł { 8, 32 , 128 }. Operational (Genie) closely tracks the theoretical upper bound, demonstrating that the global reverse-waterfilling structure of Theorem 1 is directly achievable in practice through P RISM QUANT . As K increases, the gap between the theoretical lower and upper bounds also increases due to the growing component-label cost H(C)/n , consistent with Theorem 3. Nevertheless, even at K = 128 , the gap remains below 0.1 bits/dimension, suggesting 8(a) Outdoor map 8     8   

Additional experiments with varying source dimension n are provided in Appendix G. 

From covariance clustering to spatial partitioning. Figure 5 (b)ÔÇô(d) provides a visual sanity check of the modeling assumption underlying P RISM QUANT : that CSI samples sharing similar second-order statistics also share similar geometry. Although the EM procedure operates purely in the covariance domain and is given no positional information, the resulting MAP assignments form spatially contiguous regions. Increasing the mixture order K splits each region into finer sub-regions; from a compression standpoint, this sharpens the rateÔÇôdistortion behavior at the cost of a larger dictionary. 

CSI compression on DeepMIMO. We evaluate P RISM QUANT on the DeepMIMO ÔÇśO1ÔÇÖ outdoor scenario at 28 GHz [ 14 ], generated via ray-tracing in Remcom Wireless InSite; the deployment map is shown in Figure 5(a). We configure the system with Nt=32 antennas and Nc=32 OFDM subcarriers, and retain the 38 ,298 users closest to the BS to avoid near-zero-power channels dominating the empirical distribution. The dataset is split into training and test sets with an 80 /20 ratio, and all CSI matrices are normalized to unit power following standard neural CSI-feedback practice [ 39 ]. A 9Table 1: Model size and FLOPs on DeepMIMO. FLOPs measure the computational cost of compress-ing and reconstructing one CSI realization. Since P RISM QUANT ÔÇÖs complexity and FLOPs depend on the operating rate, we report values averaged over the rate range 0ÔÇô0.5 bits per dimension.

K=16 Gaussian mixture is learned by EM on the training split and then frozen for inference. We compare P RISM QUANT against single-covariance TC, Swin-NTC [ 11 ], and CsiNet [ 10 ]. Figure 6 and Table 1 show that P RISM QUANT consistently outperforms single-covariance TC, with the gap widening at higher rates due to improved modeling of the conditional source distribution. Compared with learned baselines, P RISM QUANT continues to improve at moderate-to-high rates while Swin-NTC and CsiNet saturate. At 0.4 bpp, P RISM QUANT achieves 15 dB and 10 dB lower NMSE than Swin-NTC (1x) and CsiNet (1x), respectively, while requiring 74 .5├Ś and 1.8├Ś lower computational complexity at matched parameter budgets. Figure 6(b) further shows that increasing the mixture order K consistently improves RD performance by capturing the multimodal source distribution more accurately. 

7 Conclusion 

We presented P RISM QUANT , a constructive transform-coding framework for Gaussian-mixture sources. By augmenting the source with the latent component label, we derived an explicit converseÔÇô achievability sandwich with gap H(C)/n and showed that the entire multimodal RD problem is governed by a single global reverse-waterfilling level shared across all components and eigenmodes. We further developed a fully practical coding pipeline based on EM-driven Gaussian-mixture learn-ing, MAP component inference, component-matched KLTs, and ECSQ. Experiments on synthetic Gaussian mixtures demonstrated that P RISM QUANT closely approaches the theoretical RD bound, while experiments on the DeepMIMO CSI dataset showed competitive or superior performance compared with transformer-based learned codecs at two orders of magnitude smaller model size and complexity. Extending the experimental validation to broader multimodal source classes remains an important direction for future work. 10 References

The interpretation is that the multimodal allocation collapses onto the same single-Gaussian reverse-waterfilling rule applied to the pooled eigenmodes weighted by ¤Çc: a mode (c, i ) is active iff ╬╗c,i > ╬╝ ,regardless of ¤Çc, while the prior enters only through the expected-rate sum.

F Comparison with Classical and Standardized Transform Coding Methods 

This appendix places P RISM QUANT within a four-tier hierarchy of transform-based source codes: classical single-covariance transform coding (TC), the learned-library Weighted Universal Transform Coder (WUTC) of Effros and Chou [13] , the standardized Adaptive Multiple Transform (AMT/MTS) of HEVC and VVC [ 22 ÔÇô 26 ], and P RISM QUANT . All four schemes share the same encoder skeletonÔÇö a library of orthogonal transforms with (possibly trivial) branch signalingÔÇöbut differ in how the library is designed, how the branch is selected, how bits are allocated, and what information-theoretic guarantees are available. We first lay out the side-by-side architectural comparison, then prove 

information-theoretically why a single global water level strictly dominates per-class water levels at every operating point, then specialize the discussion to AMT, and finally confirm the analysis on a synthetic mixture.

F.3 Adaptive Multiple Transform (AMT/MTS): the standardized counterpart 

The library-of-transforms principle also appears in the HEVC and VVC video-coding standards under the name adaptive multiple transform (AMT), later renamed multiple transform selection (MTS). The AMT concept was first proposed by Han et al. [22 , 23] as a joint optimization of spatial prediction and block transform; Saxena and Fernandes [24] extended it into a mode-dependent DCT/DST 16 scheme, Zhao et al. [25] consolidated the design as enhanced multiple transform, and Bross et al. [26] documents its current form in VVC. The library is a small set of fixed trigonometric transforms 

(DCT-II together with DST-VII and DCT-VIII variants), the branch is chosen by an encoder-side rateÔÇôdistortion search and signaled with a few context-coded bits, and the bit allocation is governed by a per-block quantization parameter from the standard. AMT thus sits next to WUTC in the design space: both transmit a branch index and apply a per-branch transform plus scalar quantization, but AMT trades content-adaptivity for standardization, fast trigonometric implementations, and hardware friendliness. It uses neither a learned library nor a probabilistic source model, and its rateÔÇôdistortion behavior is documented only operationally, with no analogue of the strict-dominance argument of ┬ž(C). P RISM QUANT differs from AMT in the same three respects that distinguish it from WUTC: (i) the library is derived from a Gaussian-mixture source model instead of being hand-designed; (ii) the branch is chosen by a Bayes-optimal MAP classifier instead of by an RD search; and (iii) the bit allocation is summarized by a single global water level with a matching closed-form converse, instead of by per-block quantization parameters without an information-theoretic gap analysis.

F.5 Take-away 

The structural advantage of P RISM QUANT is information-theoretic, not algorithmic. WUTC and PRISM QUANT share the same encoderÔÇôdecoder skeleton, but WUTCÔÇÖs per-class bit budget is a 

strictly tighter constraint than P RISM QUANT ÔÇÖs single total-rate constraint, and tighter constraints produce weakly larger optimal distortion. AMT/MTS faces the same structural gap from the opposite direction: its transform library and quantizer are fixed and standardized rather than data-driven, so it cannot match either the per-class adaptivity of WUTC or the cross-class joint allocation of PRISM QUANT . Single-covariance TC inherits both losses. The only way to recover the slack is to merge all per-class budgets into a single global one over a data-driven transform libraryÔÇöwhich is exactly what P RISM QUANT does, and what Theorem 1 proves to be optimal. 17 0 

Figure 9: Effect of block length n on DeepMIMO at varying mixture order K. Together with Figure 6 (b) ( n = 1024 ), these results show that for any fixed K, increasing n consistently improves RD performance across the entire rate range. 

G Additional Experiments 

G.1 Effect of n on synthetic mixtures 

Figure 8 shows the RD performance of P RISM QUANT on synthetic Gaussian-mixture sources with fixed mixture order K = 16 and varying source dimension n Ôłł { 4, 16 , 64 }. As the source dimension increases, the gap between the theoretical lower and upper bounds progressively shrinks, consistent with Corollary 1, since the amortized component-label cost decreases with n. Moreover, both the genie-aided and MAP-based implementations closely approach the theoretical upper bound, demonstrating that practical P RISM QUANT nearly attains the predicted RD performance. Interestingly, in the low-dimensional regime ( n = 4 ), the MAP-based implementation slightly outperforms the genie-aided counterpart. This occurs because, when the mixture components are heavily overlapped relative to the source dimension, applying a component-matched KLT using a nearby estimated component can occasionally yield a more favorable RD trade-off than conditioning on the true generating component. 

G.2 Effect of n on DeepMIMO 

Figures 6 (b) and 9 show the RD performance of P RISM QUANT on the DeepMIMO dataset with fixed mixture order K and varying block length n Ôłł { 256 , 1024 , 2048 }. Partitioning the channel into blocks of size n implicitly assumes that distinct blocks are statistically independent; since CSI is in fact strongly correlated across dimensions, smaller n introduces a mismatch with the true distribution, while larger n allows the mixture to capture longer-range dependencies and yields a 18 tighter approximation of the underlying source. As a result, for any fixed mixture order K, increasing 

n consistently shifts the RD curve downward across the entire rate range, demonstrating that more faithful distributional modeling directly translates into improved compression performance. 19
